personality_voice.book_baseline: The book corpus establishes Sirius's personality almost entirely through indirect evidence: his bedroom at Grimmauld Place (Gryffindor banners to spite his Slytherin family, Muggle motorcycle posters, bikini-clad Muggle girls, Permanent Sticking Charms - all acts of deliberate, cheerful rebellion), his youthful appearance in the Marauder photograph ('carelessly handsome, his slightly arrogant face so much younger and happier'), his teenage behavior on the Hogwarts Express (sharp, witty, immediately mocking Snape with 'Where are you heading, seeing as you're neither?'), and his resurrection scene where he 'loped with an easy grace, his hands in his pockets and a grin on his face.' The books consistently portray Sirius as irreverent, bold, casually charismatic, with a streak of arrogance and a deep capacity for warmth toward those he loves. He is also shown to have real flaws: his cruelty to Kreacher (acknowledged by Hermione and Dumbledore), his recklessness, and his tendency toward impulsiveness. His voice in the Deathly Hallows resurrection scene is brief but characteristic - direct, reassuring, unafraid ('Quicker and easier than falling asleep').
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus contains only one scene where Sirius speaks: the Forbidden Forest resurrection sequence. He has two lines: 'Quicker than falling asleep' and 'No. We're here, you see' (gesturing to Harry's heart). The second line replaces the book's 'We are part of you' with a more sentimental, physically demonstrative gesture. There is no other film material in this corpus to evaluate his personality, voice, humor, arrogance, warmth, or flaws.
personality_voice.difference: The corpus is too limited to evaluate personality and voice with any confidence. The two film lines are broadly consistent with the book's Sirius - direct, calm, unafraid - but the replacement of 'We are part of you' with a gesture toward Harry's heart slightly softens the metaphysical precision of the book line into something more conventionally sentimental. The entire personality architecture established in the books (the rebellious bedroom, the sharp wit on the train, the easy grace, the flawed treatment of Kreacher) has no film equivalent in this corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The corpus is insufficient to score this dimension with confidence. The two available lines are not inconsistent with book Sirius, but they represent a vanishingly small sample. The score reflects the near-total absence of evaluable film material rather than active contradiction - but the framework requires penalizing when the available material is too limited to confirm the characterization, and the one detectable change (gesture replacing 'We are part of you') is a minor softening.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In the Deathly Hallows book corpus, Sirius functions in several ways: (1) as an emotional anchor for Harry - the broken mirror shard, the motorcycle, the bedroom, the letter from Lily all serve as grief objects that define Harry's emotional state; (2) as a structural presence whose death continues to generate consequences (Kreacher's betrayal, the house at Grimmauld Place, the inheritance); (3) as a moral reference point - Harry's anger at Lupin for 'stepping into Sirius's shoes,' Hermione's indictment of Sirius's treatment of Kreacher, Dumbledore's posthumous judgment; (4) as a resurrection figure who accompanies Harry into the forest, providing courage and presence. In the resurrection scene specifically, Sirius speaks, reassures, and gestures - he is an active participant, not merely decorative.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film corpus, Sirius appears only in the resurrection scene. He speaks two lines and makes one gesture. His narrative function as grief object (mirror shard, motorcycle, bedroom) is entirely absent from the film corpus. His role in the Kreacher subplot - which in the books is directly tied to his death and his failure as a master - has no presence here. His function as a moral reference point (the Lupin confrontation, Hermione's indictment) is absent.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film corpus strips Sirius of all narrative agency except his brief participation in the resurrection scene. His role as the emotional and moral weight behind multiple subplots (Kreacher, Grimmauld Place, Harry's grief, the Lupin confrontation) is entirely absent from the available film material. Even in the resurrection scene, his agency is minimal - James Potter takes the most active lines ('You're nearly there, son,' 'Until the end'), while Sirius is reduced to two short reassurances.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer rule applies: in the resurrection scene, James Potter takes the most prominent active role ('You're nearly there, son,' 'Until the end'), which in the books is more evenly distributed. Sirius's broader narrative agency across the book - as the source of Harry's grief, the cause of Kreacher's betrayal, the moral counterexample - is entirely absent from the film corpus. This is a severe agency reduction.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Not enough evidence - the corpus is too limited to evaluate the full scope of Sirius's narrative role in the films, but what is present shows significant reduction.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sirius's motivations in the Deathly Hallows book corpus are present only through posthumous traces: his love for Harry (the mirror, the broomstick gift in Lily's letter, the bedroom photograph), his rebellious identity (the bedroom decor), and his flawed relationship with Kreacher (which Hermione and Dumbledore both explicitly analyze as stemming from Sirius's inability to see Kreacher as a being with feelings). In the resurrection scene, his motivation is simply to accompany and support Harry - no internal conflict is present or expected at this stage.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film corpus provides no material to evaluate Sirius's motivations beyond the resurrection scene, where his motivation (to support Harry) is consistent with the books. The complex posthumous portrait of his motivations - his love for Harry, his rebellious identity, his failure with Kreacher - is entirely absent from the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The motivational complexity established in the books through posthumous traces (the bedroom, Lily's letter, Kreacher's testimony, Dumbledore's judgment) has no equivalent in the film corpus. The resurrection scene motivation is consistent but trivially simple.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The corpus is too limited to evaluate this dimension properly. The available film material is not contradictory, but the rich motivational portrait built through posthumous traces in the books is entirely absent. Score reflects the absence of evaluable material and the consequent inability to confirm the characterization.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sirius's arc in Deathly Hallows is entirely retrospective - he is dead throughout. His arc is traced through objects (the mirror, the motorcycle, the bedroom, Lily's letter), through other characters' judgments (Hermione's indictment of his treatment of Kreacher, Dumbledore's posthumous observation), and through his appearance via the Resurrection Stone. The arc that matters here is the completed arc from the earlier books: the wrongly imprisoned man who became Harry's godfather, who was reckless and flawed but deeply loving, and who died because of those flaws. In Deathly Hallows, this arc is honored through grief and through the resurrection scene, where Sirius appears 'younger by far than Harry had seen him in life,' 'loped with an easy grace,' suggesting a kind of peace or restoration.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film corpus contains only the resurrection scene. Sirius appears, speaks two lines, and gestures. There is no material to evaluate whether the film honors or distorts the completed arc.
character_arc.difference: The retrospective arc - honored in the books through the bedroom, the letter, the motorcycle, the Kreacher subplot, and the resurrection - is almost entirely absent from the film corpus. The resurrection scene alone cannot carry the weight of the arc.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The corpus is too limited to evaluate the arc dimension. The resurrection scene is consistent with the arc's endpoint (peace, love, support for Harry), but the journey that makes that endpoint meaningful is absent from the film corpus.

key_relationships.book_baseline: The book corpus establishes three key relationships for Sirius in Deathly Hallows: (1) Sirius and Harry - the central relationship, expressed through grief objects (mirror, motorcycle, bedroom, Lily's letter), through Harry's anger at Lupin for trying to replace Sirius, and through the resurrection scene where Sirius accompanies Harry to his death; (2) Sirius and Kreacher - a deeply flawed relationship whose failure caused Sirius's death and whose consequences drive the Grimmauld Place subplot; (3) Sirius and the Marauders (James, Lupin, Pettigrew) - shown in the photograph and in the resurrection scene. The Sirius-Harry relationship is the most important and is characterized by love, grief, and the weight of loss.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows only the resurrection scene. The Sirius-Harry dynamic is present but compressed: Sirius speaks two lines and gestures toward Harry's heart. The Sirius-Kreacher relationship is entirely absent. The Sirius-Marauder dynamic is present in the resurrection scene but undifferentiated - all four figures appear together with roughly equal weight.
key_relationships.difference: The Sirius-Kreacher relationship, which in the books is a major source of moral complexity and narrative consequence, is entirely absent from the film corpus. The Sirius-Harry relationship is present but reduced to two lines and a gesture, with James Potter taking the more prominent emotional role in the scene ('You're nearly there, son,' 'Until the end'). The emotional weight of the relationship - built through grief objects and Harry's fierce protectiveness of Sirius's memory - has no film equivalent in this corpus.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The transfer rule applies: James Potter takes the most emotionally prominent lines in the resurrection scene, partially displacing Sirius's role in the Sirius-Harry relationship. The Sirius-Kreacher relationship is entirely absent. These are significant losses that change how the character is understood.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Not enough evidence - the corpus is too limited, but what is present shows the Sirius-Harry dynamic is partially transferred to James.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book corpus is extraordinarily rich in posthumous complexity for Sirius. His bedroom reveals a fully realized personality: the deliberate rebellion against his family, the Muggle interests, the permanent sticking charms as acts of defiance. Lily's letter reveals his role as Harry's first broomstick giver and his place in the Potters' lives. The Kreacher subplot reveals his most significant flaw - his inability to treat Kreacher as a being with feelings, which Hermione explicitly connects to his death. The Lupin confrontation reveals how Harry's grief for Sirius shapes his moral judgments. The resurrection scene shows him at peace, younger, graceful. The reference to Bellatrix's laugh being 'the same exhilarated laugh her cousin Sirius had given as he toppled backward through the veil' retroactively adds a disturbing complexity to his death. All of this builds a character who is simultaneously heroic, flawed, beloved, and morally implicated.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film corpus contains two lines and a gesture in the resurrection scene. None of the posthumous complexity - the bedroom, the letter, the Kreacher subplot, the Lupin confrontation, the Bellatrix parallel - is present in the film corpus.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The entire architecture of posthumous complexity that makes Sirius one of the richest absent characters in Deathly Hallows is absent from the film corpus. What remains is a brief, warm, uncomplicated presence in the resurrection scene.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is the most severely affected dimension. The film corpus removes essentially all of the nuance, contradiction, and complexity that defines Sirius in the books. The flawed treatment of Kreacher, the rebellious identity, the grief objects, the moral weight of his death - none of this is present. The character is reduced to a reassuring ghost.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Not enough evidence - but the absence itself is the evidence.

lost_or_transferred_material: Sirius's bedroom at Grimmauld Place as a character-defining space (Gryffindor banners, Muggle posters, Permanent Sticking Charms) - absent from film corpus
Lily's letter establishing Sirius as Harry's first broomstick giver and intimate friend of the Potters - absent from film corpus
The Kreacher subplot and Sirius's posthumous moral indictment for his treatment of Kreacher - absent from film corpus
Harry's confrontation with Lupin ('You fancy stepping into Sirius's shoes') as an expression of Harry's grief and Sirius's irreplaceability - absent from film corpus
Dumbledore's posthumous judgment: 'I do not think Sirius ever saw Kreacher as a being with feelings as acute as a human's' - absent from film corpus
The Marauder photograph in Sirius's bedroom showing him 'carelessly handsome, his slightly arrogant face so much younger and happier' - absent from film corpus
The parallel between Bellatrix's laugh and Sirius's laugh as he fell through the veil - absent from film corpus
Harry sleeping in Sirius's room and studying the Marauder photograph - absent from film corpus
The mirror shard as grief object carried throughout the book - absent from film corpus
James Potter taking the most prominent emotional lines in the resurrection scene ('You're nearly there, son,' 'Until the end'), partially displacing Sirius's role in the Sirius-Harry relationship
Sirius's line 'We are part of you' replaced by a gesture toward Harry's heart - minor softening of the metaphysical precision of the book
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 not permitted): an action and emotional beat from the book - James Potter taking 'Until the end' and 'You're nearly there, son' - is transferred from the collective Sirius-James dynamic to James alone
Cap 1 (score above 85 not permitted): the films remove a major part of Sirius's complexity, agency, key relationships, and narrative function as established in the books
Cap 4 (score above 70 not permitted): the films preserve only Sirius's broad identity (he appears, he is warm, he is unafraid of death) while removing most of what defines his book characterization
key_observations: This evaluation is severely constrained by corpus limitations. The book corpus is rich with posthumous Sirius material - his bedroom, Lily's letter, the Kreacher subplot, the Lupin confrontation, grief objects, moral indictments - while the film corpus contains only two lines and a gesture in the resurrection scene. The score of 39 reflects not active contradiction of the book character (the two film lines are broadly consistent with book Sirius) but the near-total absence of the material that makes Sirius complex, flawed, and narratively significant in Deathly Hallows. The one detectable adaptation change - James Potter taking the most prominent emotional lines in the resurrection scene - partially displaces Sirius's role in his most important relationship. All confidence levels are Low because the film corpus is too limited to evaluate any dimension with security; the scores are tentative assessments based on what is absent rather than what is present.
